<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erat_optandum_1">
								<gloss>what
							was chiefly to be wished:</gloss> not implying a protasis contrary to
							fact. See § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (311, c); B. 304, 3; G. 254,
							R.1; H. 583 (511, 1, N.8); H.-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_pertinebat_1">
								<gloss>the
							one thing which most tended</gloss> (or, <gloss>was of
								chief importance).</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiam_infamiamque_1">
								<gloss>odium
							and ill repute</gloss>, from the partisan use of the courts by the
							Senators. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vestri_ordinis_1">,
							i.e. the senatorial order. The word <foreign xml:lang="lat">ordo</foreign>
							signified, loosely, any recognized body of citizens—as freedmen,
							publicans, clerks; but it was more especially used of the two powerful
							classes of the Roman aristocracy, the Senatorial and the Equestrian,
							which struggled with each other for power during the last century of the
							Republic. The Senators, from whom the jurors were at this time taken
							(see note on Rosc. Am., p. 2,1. l), formed a limited (300 to 600) order
							of nobility which virtually controlled the government. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> constituted a moneyed aristocracy.
							Naturally these two orders had opposing interests, as the Senators were
							excluded from trade and the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> practically from political power.
							Their antagonism showed itself more especially in the matter of the
							provinces, which the Senators wished to oppress by official plunder and
							the <foreign xml:lang="lat">equites</foreign> by commercial extortion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="summo_tempore_1">,
							most <gloss>critical time</gloss> (more lit. <gloss>extreme</gloss>
							crisis): the year of the consulship of Pompey and Crassus (<date when="-0070">B.C. 70</date>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inveteravit_1">
							(emphatic position), <gloss>there has come to be deeply
								rooted</gloss> (observe that the figure is quite different in the
							Latin). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opinio_1"> , <gloss>
							notion</gloss> or <gloss>idea</gloss> (not so strong as our <gloss>
							opinion</gloss>, which should be <foreign xml:lang="lat">sententia</foreign>
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exteras_nationes_1">the
							reference is, of course, to the peoples subject to Rome, who were
							aggrieved by the rapacity of the provincial governors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_iudiciis_1">:
							in consequence of the situation described above (note on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ordinis_1">,
							l. 2) it became all important for one class or the other to control the
							courts, before which any misdoings of either party were likely to come
							for trial. For years these two orders had struggled for such control. At
							this particular time the courts were in the hands of the Senators, who
							were bound together by a common cause to shield any one of their number
							who might be charged with misconduct as a provincial governor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neminem_1">
							(more emphatic than <foreign xml:lang="lat">nullum</foreign>) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_1">
							translate, <gloss>never.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_2">
							(causal) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sint_2"> , <gloss>when
							men are ready.—</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contionibus_legibus_2">
								<gloss>harangues
							and bills</gloss> (proposed laws). The proposition of a law which took
							the exclusive control of the courts from the Senators was even now
							pending, and the law (<foreign xml:lang="lat">Lex Aurelia</foreign>) was
							passed before the case of Verres was decided. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="conentur_2">,
							purpose clause. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magnitudine_2">,
							abl. of means. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spe_2">,
							abl. of specification. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="actor_2"> , <gloss>
							complainant</gloss>, i.e. agent or <gloss>attorney</gloss> for
							conducting the suit in personal processes (<gloss>in personam</gloss> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adduxi_hominem_2">etc.:
							cf. Burke, Impeachment of Warren Hastings: <q rend="double">We have
							brought before your Lordships the first man in property and power;
								we have brought before you the head, the chief, the
								captain-general
								in iniquity,—one in whom all the frauds, all the peculations,
								all
								the tyranny in India are embodied, disciplined, and arrayed.
								Then,
								if we have brought before you such a person, if you strike at
								him,
								you will not have need of a great many more examples,— you
								strike at
								the whole corps if you strike at the head:</q>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_quo_2"> , <gloss>in
							whose case</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reconciliare_2">
							etc., <gloss>win back the lost repute.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possetis_2">,
							purpose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="depeculatorem_2">etc.:
							for a more complete statement of these charges, see chs. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.4">iv</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.5">v</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="juris_urbani_2">
							i.e. <gloss>as praetor urbanus</gloss> (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.12">12</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vos_3">,
							opposed to </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ego_3">
							below. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religiose_3">
								<gloss>according to your oath.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="religionem_veritatemque_3">
							here, <gloss>feeling of obligation and regard for the
								truth.</gloss>Notice that the Latin, having a comparatively poor
							vocabulary, is obliged to use one word for all the phrases or sides of
							an idea; hence such a word as <foreign xml:lang="lat">veritas</foreign>
							may mean <gloss>truth</gloss> (abstractly), <gloss>a truth</gloss>
							(concretely), the <gloss>truth</gloss> (generally), <gloss>regard
								for truth</gloss>, or <gloss>truthful
								conduct.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="judicium_3">,
							etc., i.e. the <gloss>court</gloss> will be found wanting,—not a
							suitable defendant or a zealous prosecutor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equidem_3">,
							i.e. for my own part. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quas_partim_3">
								<gloss>some of which.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="devitarim_3">subj.
							as a part of the concession contained in </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_sint_3">:
							§ <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324, I; G. 663, I; H. 652 (529,
							ii); H-B. 539. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_neque_3">,
							following </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="numquam_3">
							does not destroy the negative, but is more emphatic than <foreign xml:lang="lat">aut . . . aut</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istius_4">:
							see note on Rosc. Am., <ref n="pl4.16">p. 4, l. 16</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Glabrioni_4">:
							the praetor presiding. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ordini_senatorio_4">
								<gloss>the senatorial order, nay, the very name of Senator.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictitat_4">
								<gloss>constantly
							repeats:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 263">263</ref>, 2 (167, b); B. 155, 2,
							a; G. 191, I; H. 364 (336); H.-B. 212, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esse_metuendum_4">:
							for <foreign xml:lang="lat">erat met.</foreign> in dir. disc.; hence
							followed by the secondary sequence, i.e. <gloss>those
								would have to fear</gloss> (if the case were theirs), but <gloss>
								he,</gloss> etc. § <ref n="AG 584">584</ref>, a and N. (336 A,
							N.l); H.-B. 581, b, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_4">,
							i.e. only what. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multis_4">i.e.
							not only for himself but also for his counsel and for those whom he may
							wish to bribe,—in particular, the jurors (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.40">40</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pecunia_4">belongs
							to both clauses, as is shown by their parallelism. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_4">for
							tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c (287, c); B. 268, 7; G. 513;
							H. 550 (495, vi); cf. H.-B. 478. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="esset_5">imperf.
							subj. in protasis of a <gloss>continued</gloss> condition lasting till
							now; § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, a (308, a); B. 304, 2; G. 597, R.l;
							H. 579, I (510, N.2); H.-B. 581. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fefellisset_5">
								<gloss>he would have eluded us.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cadit_5">pres.
							tense, of an action lasting till now; § <ref n="AG 466">466</ref>
							(276, a); B. 259, 4; G. 230; H. 533 (467, 2); H.-B. 485. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="corrumpendi_judici_5">
							, <gloss>of bribing the court</gloss> (cf. our phrase <q rend="double">bribery
								and corruption</q>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="factus_sit_5">for
							sequence, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c, N.2 (287, C, N.); B.
							268, 6; G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); cf. H.-B. 478; notice that the perf.
							would necessarily be used in the dir. disc. with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_primum_5">
							. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempus_offenderet_5">
								<gloss>he
							hit an unfavorable time;</gloss> because popular sentiment was already
							so exasperated in regard to the corruption of the courts. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_inquirendi_6">
							i.e. <gloss>for going into Sicily to make an
								investigation</gloss> (hence the acc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invenit_qui_6">
								<gloss>he found some one who.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_Achaiam_6">,
							sc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inquirendi_6">on
							this trumped-up case, which was intended to have the precedence of the
							trial of Verres, see Introd. to this Oration (p. 27). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_conficeret_6">purp.
							clause dependent on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invenit_6">
						</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Brundisium_6">
								<gloss>
							Brindisi</gloss>,the port whence the greater part of Italian travel, now
							as then, embarks for the East. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obii_6"> ,<gloss>went
							throughout</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="populorum_6">
							,<gloss>commmunities:</gloss> the word <foreign xml:lang="lat">
								populus, </foreign>meaning originally <gloss>multitude</gloss>, is a
							semi-abstract noun often used to denote the community in its official
							capacity Our use of the word <gloss>people</gloss> in some later
							meanings frequently produces confusion in the minds of beginners. The
							political system of the ancients was composed of an indefinite number of
							petty communities, all possessing a certain degree of independence.
							Hence the plur. is used here to indicate several such communities. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_posset_6">
							(clause of result), imperf by seq. of tenses: translate, however, <gloss>
							can.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_obsideret_6">
							(purpose), <gloss>to block my chance</gloss> (of bringing Verres to
							trial). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_7">i.e.
							now that his former scheme has failed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_7"> ,<gloss>this
							new idea.</gloss> What the idea is is detailed in sects. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.7">7</ref>,<ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.8">8</ref>: viz. the reasons for desiring a
							postponement together with grounds for hoping for it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_7"> ,<gloss>
							citizens</gloss>, i.e. Romans travelling or doing business in the
							provinces, or provincials who had received the citizenship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="socios_7">
								<gloss>
							allies:</gloss> citizens of communities which, although embraced within
							the boundaries of Roman provinces, had, for special reasons, been
							allowed to retain a nominal independence, with their own laws and
							magistrates. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctoritatibus_7">
								<gloss>
							documents</gloss>, i.e. official testimony (<q rend="double">
							resolutions,</q> etc.) relating to the acts of Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bonis_8"> ,<gloss>good
							citizens:</gloss> here, as generally in Cicero, used in a partisan sense
							for the aristocracy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="experiatur_8">this
							violates the sequence of tenses in order to make the meaning clear; the
							imperf. would refer to the time of getting the money, not to the present
							moment; cf. § <ref n="AG 481">481</ref>, N. (287, h, N.); B. 268, 7;
							G. 509, 1, N.; H.-B. 478. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuerit_8">:
							the subj. shows that this is the thought of Verres, and not merely
							something thrown in by Cicero. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tempus_8">:
							the present scheme of the defence is by corrupt means to stave off the
							trial to a more advantageous time (see chs. vi-viii). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="posses_8">imperf.
							to express his purpose at the time of the purchase. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="criminum_vim_8">
							,<gloss>the force of the charges.</gloss>
							</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="poterat_8">:indic.,
							the reason being Cicero’s. (The whole passage is an instructive example
							of the freedom of a living language from its own trammels. Rules are
							made for language, not language for rules.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eloquentia_gratia_9">even
							mere rhetorical skill or personal influence would be, to a criminal who
							had no case </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causa_9">
							a respectable (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="honesto_9">)
							means of escape compared with these attempts at corruption. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profecto_9">
							,<gloss>I am sure</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aucuparetur_9">
								<gloss>be
							fishing for</gloss> (lit. <gloss>set nets for
								birds</gloss>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_fieret_9">
								<gloss>as
							to have some one chosen to be put on trial</gloss> ; the Senate itself
							was insulted by the selection of one of its members to be set up as a
							man of straw, that Verres might get clear. The reference is to the
							trumped-up case with regard to abuses in Achaia (see sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.6">6</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_9">,
							i.e. Verres. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causam_diceret_9">
								<gloss>stand
							trial</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_rebus_10">
							,<gloss>from this</gloss> (abl. of means with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perspicio_10">
							). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilio_10">
							,<gloss>panel</gloss>, i.e. the body of jurors (cf. Rosc. Am., p. 24, I.
							2). An obvious, and apparently a deserved, compliment. Whatever the
							general character of the courts, Cicero had in this instance secured a
							jury on whom he could rely. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_judicavit_10">
								<gloss>decided
							at the challenging</gloss> (<q rend="double">throwing out</q>) <gloss>of
							the jury</gloss>, i.e. on seeing the kind of men challenged by the two
							sides respectively. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_arbitraretur_10">subst.
							clauses of result (justified by the introductory </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ea_10">)
							instead of the more regular acc. and inf. of ind. disc.; § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>, c (332,f); <gloss>G. 557</gloss>, R. and N1;
							cf. H. 571,4 (501, iii). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etenim_10">introducing
							the reason of </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullam_rem_10">,
							etc., above. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adulescentiae_11">i.e.
							before he entered public life. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaestura_11">
								<gloss>
							quaestorship</gloss>, the first grade of political honor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Carbonem_11">Carbo
							was the leader of the Marian faction after the death of Marius and
							Cinna. He was consul <date when="-0082">B.C. 82</date>, the year of
							Sulla’s return and victory. Verres was his quaestor (or paymaster), and
							went over to the enemy with the money-chest when he saw which side was
							likely to prevail. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necessitudinem_religionemque_11">the
							quaestor was originally nominated specially by the consul; and the
							peculiarly close and sacred relation (<foreign xml:lang="lat">
							necessitudo</foreign>) existing between them was known as <foreign xml:lang="lat">pietas</foreign>,—a sentiment akin to filial
							affection. The designation by lot (<foreign xml:lang="lat">sors</foreign>)
							was also held to be a token of divine will, and therefore sacred (<foreign xml:lang="lat">religio</foreign>). In betraying his consul, then,
							Verres was guilty of more than an ordinary breach of trust,—he committed
							an act of impiety. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="legatio_11">Verres
							was in <date from="-0080" to="-0079">
									<date when="-0080">B.C. 80</date>-79</date>
								<foreign xml:lang="lat">legatus</foreign> and acting quaestor (</gloss>
